interpretations.title: Results and Interpretations
rows: Indicates healthy status; no further action typically needed
Requires clinical follow-up to monitor progression or improvement
Indicative of bone injury; further management may include immobilization, surgery, or physical therapy
Requires urgent medical attention; anticoagulation therapy often prescribed

faqs.question: Is a CT LEG scan painful?
faqs.answer: No. It’s completely painless, though you may feel a brief warm sensation if contrast is used.

faqs.question: How soon can I get results?
faqs.answer: Routine scans: within 6 hours. Emergency scans: preliminary report in 30 minutes.

faqs.question: Can I drive home after the scan?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless you received sedation, which is rare for leg imaging.

faqs.question: Does insurance cover the test?
faqs.answer: Most policies cover medically indicated scans. Cadabams Diagnostics offers cashless approval within 30 minutes.

faqs.question: Why might I need a leg CT scan with contrast?
faqs.answer: Contrast material is used to make certain structures "light up" on the scan. It is essential for getting a better view of blood vessels (to check for clots or blockages), evaluating for tumors, and identifying collections of infection (abscesses).

faqs.question: What if I’m claustrophobic?
faqs.answer: Our wide-bore scanner and open design reduce anxiety. Music headphones are also provided.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

seo.title: Leg CT Scan: Purpose, Procedure & Results Guide
seo.description: Learn everything about CT LEG scans at Cadabams Diagnostics—what to expect, preparation tips, risks, and how results help diagnose fractures, DVT, PAD and more.

test.markdown:
## Leg CT Scan Overview

### What to Expect During Your Leg CT Scan
- Warm welcome at the front desk and quick registration
- A short wait in a calm, air-conditioned lounge
- A technologist explains the scan and answers questions
- Actual scanning takes 5–15 minutes; you remain fully clothed unless contrast is used
- Immediate post-scan review to confirm image quality
### Why Choose Cadabams Diagnostics for Leg Imaging
- 128-slice CT scanner for sub-millimetre clarity
- Same-day or next-day appointments, including weekends
- On-site radiologists for urgent reads
- Transparent pricing with no hidden costs
- Free parking and wheelchair access throughout the facility
## 2. What Is a Leg CT Scan?
### How the Leg CT Scan Works
A CT LEG exam uses a rotating X-ray tube and detectors to capture hundreds of cross-sectional images. Powerful software stitches these slices into detailed 3-D reconstructions, allowing doctors to inspect bones, muscles, fat, and blood vessels layer by layer.
### Difference Between CT Scan and CT Angiography of Legs
| \*\*Standard CT LEG\*\* | \*\*CT Angiography Legs\*\* |
|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Focuses on bones and soft tissues | Highlights arteries and veins |
| No contrast or optional contrast | Requires iodinated contrast |
| Screens fractures, tumours | Detects PAD, DVT, aneurysms |
## When to Take Test
### Common Symptoms That Require Leg CT Scan
- Sudden swelling or redness
- Persistent leg pain with no clear cause
- Deformed limb after injury
- Non-healing ulcers on feet or ankles
- Cold, pale foot suggesting poor blood flow
### Age Groups and Risk Factors
- Adults 40+ with diabetes or hypertension
- Athletes with recurrent stress injuries
- Smokers or those with high cholesterol
- Post-surgical patients monitoring grafts or hardware

## Risks & Limitations of the CT Leg Scan

### Radiation Exposure Concerns
The effective dose for a CT LEG scan is roughly 2–4 mSv—similar to eight months of natural background radiation. Our low-dose protocols reduce exposure by up to 30 % without sacrificing image quality.
### Contrast Dye Side Effects
- Mild warmth or metallic taste (common, harmless)
- Nausea or rash (<1 %)
- Severe allergic reaction (<0.05 %)
### Who Should Avoid This Test
- Pregnant women (radiation risk to fetus)
- Patients with severe iodine allergy
- Individuals with eGFR <30 and contrast exam (risk of nephrotoxicity)
## 5. Types of Leg CT Scan
### Standard Leg CT Scan
Captures bones, joints, and surrounding soft tissues. Ideal for trauma and chronic pain.
### CT Angiography Legs
Contrast-enhanced scan of arteries from the pelvis to the foot. Detects blockages in peripheral artery disease (PAD).
### CT Venography
Maps the deep veins to diagnose deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and varicose vein sources.

## List of Parameters Considered During the CT Leg Scan

- \*\*Bone density measurements\*\* – evaluate osteoporosis or fracture healing
- \*\*Soft tissue evaluation\*\* – check muscle tears, abscesses, or tumours
- \*\*Vascular patency assessment\*\* – ensure arteries and veins are open

## Reasons for Taking a CT Leg Scan

### Trauma and Injury Assessment
Quickly identifies complex fractures, joint dislocations, and foreign bodies after accidents.
### Chronic Leg Pain Evaluation
Uncovers occult stress fractures, arthritis, or soft-tissue lesions missed on X-rays.
### Pre-Surgical Planning
3-D images guide orthopaedic surgeons in choosing plate sizes, screw angles, or bypass graft length.

## Benefits of Taking the Test

- \*\*Accurate diagnosis\*\* – pinpoints problems early, preventing complications
- \*\*Non-invasive procedure\*\* – no incisions or anaesthesia needed
- \*\*Detailed 3-D imaging\*\* – rotate, zoom, and slice images to view every an

## Illnesses Diagnosed with CT Leg Scan

- \*\*Fractures and bone injuries\*\* – simple, compound, or hairline cracks
- \*\*Deep vein thrombosis (DVT)\*\* – blood clots in deep veins
- \*\*Peripheral artery disease (PAD)\*\* – narrowed or blocked leg arteries
- \*\*Soft tissue tumours\*\* – lipomas, sarcomas, or cysts

## Preparing for the CT Leg Scan

- \*\*Fasting\*\* – 3–4 hours if IV contrast is planned; clear fluids allowed
- \*\*Clothing and accessories\*\* – wear metal-free attire or change into hospital gown; remove jewellery
- \*\*Medication considerations\*\* – continue most meds; hold metformin on the day of contrast scan
## 11. Pre-requisites
- \*\*Doctor's prescription\*\* – mandatory for insurance and scheduling
- \*\*Medical history review\*\* – allergies, kidney disease, pregnancy status
- \*\*Kidney function tests\*\* – serum creatinine within past 4 weeks if contrast is required
## 12. Best Time to Take the Leg CT Scan
- \*\*Morning appointments\*\* – ideal for fasting patients to minimise hunger and delays
- \*\*Emergency cases\*\* – 24/7 availability for trauma or suspected DVT
- \*\*Scheduled follow-up scans\*\* – coordinate with orthopaedic clinic visits for convenience
## 13. Eligibility
- \*\*Age criteria\*\* – infants to seniors (paediatric protocols available)
- \*\*Pregnancy considerations\*\* – only if life-saving and after risk-benefit counselling
- \*\*Weight limitations\*\* – scanner accommodates up to 200 kg; larger patients may need open MRI alternative
## 14. Procedure for Taking a Leg CT Scan
### Check-In Process
- Present ID and prescription at reception
- Complete brief questionnaire on allergies and implants
### During the Scan
- \*\*Positioning\*\* – lie supine on the table; leg immobilised with soft straps
- \*\*Duration\*\* – 5–15 minutes depending on scan type
- \*\*Instructions\*\* – hold breath for a few seconds during certain phases
### Post-Scan Instructions
- Resume normal diet and activities immediately
- Drink extra water if contrast was given to flush kidneys
- Collect printed films or access digital report via Cadabams Diagnostics patient portal
## 15. Caution Before Taking the Test
- \*\*Contrast allergy screening\*\* – disclose any prior reactions to iodine or shellfish
- \*\*Pregnancy test requirements\*\* – women of child-bearing age (12–50 years) may need a quick urine test
